title: Enable All Function Properties when Stats are enabled
author: InakiVA
description: We want to track the behavior of all of these stats over time, but they are currently locked behind being run with the enabled detailed function properties flag, which does not allow us to look at their behavior unless everyone building enables the flag. This way we will always have the metrics visible whenever stats are enabled

tests.test_body:
define i32 @main() {
entry:
  %retval = alloca i32, align 4
  %mat1 = alloca [2 x [2 x i32]], align 16
  %mat2 = alloca [2 x [2 x i32]], align 16
  %res = alloca [2 x [2 x i32]], align 16
  %i = alloca i32, align 4
  %j = alloca i32, align 4
  store i32 0, ptr %retval, align 4
  %arraydecay = getelementptr inbounds [2 x [2 x i32]], ptr %mat1, i64 0, i64 0
  %arraydecay1 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x [2 x i32]], ptr %mat2, i64 0, i64 0
  %arraydecay2 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x [2 x i32]], ptr %res, i64 0, i64 0
  call void @multiply(ptr %arraydecay, ptr %arraydecay1, ptr %arraydecay2)
  ret i32 0
}

declare void @multiply(ptr, ptr, ptr)

define void @multiply(ptr %mat1, ptr %mat2, ptr %res) {
entry:
  %mat1.addr = alloca ptr, align 8
  %mat2.addr = alloca ptr, align 8
  %res.addr = alloca ptr, align 8
  %i = alloca i32, align 4
  %j = alloca i32, align 4
  %k = alloca i32, align 4
  store ptr %mat1, ptr %mat1.addr, align 8
  store ptr %mat2, ptr %mat2.addr, align 8
  store ptr %res, ptr %res.addr, align 8
  store i32 0, ptr %i, align 4
  br label %for.cond

for.cond:                                         ; preds = %for.inc24, %entry
  %0 = load i32, ptr %i, align 4
  %cmp = icmp slt i32 %0, 2
  br i1 %cmp, label %for.body, label %for.end26

for.body:                                         ; preds = %for.cond
  store i32 0, ptr %j, align 4
  br label %for.cond1

for.cond1:                                        ; preds = %for.inc21, %for.body
  %1 = load i32, ptr %j, align 4
  %cmp2 = icmp slt i32 %1, 2
  br i1 %cmp2, label %for.body3, label %for.end23

for.body3:                                        ; preds = %for.cond1
  %2 = load ptr, ptr %res.addr, align 8
  %3 = load i32, ptr %i, align 4
  %idxprom = sext i32 %3 to i64
  %arrayidx = getelementptr inbounds [2 x i32], ptr %2, i64 %idxprom
  %4 = load i32, ptr %j, align 4
  %idxprom4 = sext i32 %4 to i64
  %arrayidx5 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x i32], ptr %arrayidx, i64 0, i64 %idxprom4
  store i32 0, ptr %arrayidx5, align 4
  store i32 0, ptr %k, align 4
  br label %for.cond6

for.cond6:                                        ; preds = %for.inc, %for.body3
  %5 = load i32, ptr %k, align 4
  %cmp7 = icmp slt i32 %5, 2
  br i1 %cmp7, label %for.body8, label %for.end

for.body8:                                        ; preds = %for.cond6
  %6 = load ptr, ptr %mat1.addr, align 8
  %7 = load i32, ptr %i, align 4
  %idxprom9 = sext i32 %7 to i64
  %arrayidx10 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x i32], ptr %6, i64 %idxprom9
  %8 = load i32, ptr %k, align 4
  %idxprom11 = sext i32 %8 to i64
  %arrayidx12 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x i32], ptr %arrayidx10, i64 0, i64 %idxprom11
  %9 = load i32, ptr %arrayidx12, align 4
  %10 = load ptr, ptr %mat2.addr, align 8
  %11 = load i32, ptr %k, align 4
  %idxprom13 = sext i32 %11 to i64
  %arrayidx14 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x i32], ptr %10, i64 %idxprom13
  %12 = load i32, ptr %j, align 4
  %idxprom15 = sext i32 %12 to i64
  %arrayidx16 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x i32], ptr %arrayidx14, i64 0, i64 %idxprom15
  %13 = load i32, ptr %arrayidx16, align 4
  %mul = mul nsw i32 %9, %13
  %14 = load ptr, ptr %res.addr, align 8
  %15 = load i32, ptr %i, align 4
  %idxprom17 = sext i32 %15 to i64
  %arrayidx18 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x i32], ptr %14, i64 %idxprom17
  %16 = load i32, ptr %j, align 4
  %idxprom19 = sext i32 %16 to i64
  %arrayidx20 = getelementptr inbounds [2 x i32], ptr %arrayidx18, i64 0, i64 %idxprom19
  %17 = load i32, ptr %arrayidx20, align 4
  %add = add nsw i32 %17, %mul
  store i32 %add, ptr %arrayidx20, align 4
  br label %for.inc

for.inc:                                          ; preds = %for.body8
  %18 = load i32, ptr %k, align 4
  %inc = add nsw i32 %18, 1
  store i32 %inc, ptr %k, align 4
  br label %for.cond6

for.end:                                          ; preds = %for.cond6
  br label %for.inc21

for.inc21:                                        ; preds = %for.end
  %19 = load i32, ptr %j, align 4
  %inc22 = add nsw i32 %19, 1
  store i32 %inc22, ptr %j, align 4
  br label %for.cond1

for.end23:                                        ; preds = %for.cond1
  br label %for.inc24

for.inc24:                                        ; preds = %for.end23
  %20 = load i32, ptr %i, align 4
  %inc25 = add nsw i32 %20, 1
  store i32 %inc25, ptr %i, align 4
  br label %for.cond

for.end26:                                        ; preds = %for.cond
  ret void
}

; Testing with all of the below run lines that the pass gets added to the appropriate pipelines
; REQUIRES: asserts



define void @foo(i32 %i, i32 %j, i32 %n) {
entry:
  %cmp = icmp slt i32 %i, %j
  br i1 %cmp, label %if.then, label %if.end

if.then:
  call void @f()
  br label %if.end

if.end:
  switch i32 %i, label %sw.default [
    i32 1, label %sw.bb
    i32 2, label %sw.bb1
    i32 3, label %sw.bb1
  ]

sw.bb:
  call void @g()
  br label %sw.epilog

sw.bb1:
  call void @h()
  br label %sw.epilog

sw.default:
  call void @k()
  br label %sw.epilog

sw.epilog:
  %cmp2 = icmp sgt i32 %i, %n
  br i1 %cmp2, label %if.then3, label %if.else

if.then3:
  call void @l()
  br label %if.end4

if.else:
  call void @m()
  br label %if.end4

if.end4:
  ret void
}

declare void @f()
declare void @g()
declare void @h()
declare void @k()
declare void @l()
declare void @m()

comments.author: github-actions[bot]
comments.body:
<!--LLVM CODE FORMAT COMMENT: {clang-format}-->

:white_check_mark: With the latest revision this PR passed the C/C++ code formatter.

comments.author: aeubanks
comments.body: why do we even have separate "detailed" stats? it seems simpler to treat all stats the same

comments.author: boomanaiden154
comments.body:
> why do we even have separate "detailed" stats? it seems simpler to treat all stats the same

Non-detailed stats are used by the ML inliner as features. Detailed stats are intended for use cases like this, with the idea being that it does not make sense to calculate them when this is used as an analysis. I don't think any measurement has been done on whether or not there is actual compile time overhead, but that's the theory.

comments.author: InakiVA
comments.body:
> If you're dumping the stats somewhere, you presumably have control over the compiler command line?

We are tracking all metrics of all builds, not just our own, but we cannot get that information unless everyone builds with this flag enabled when stats are enabled. It's for an analysis over time of the behavior they follow and to be able to detect which changes generated the most impact, let it be positive or negative

comments.author: aeubanks
comments.body: it'd be nice to check if this actually has any compile time overhead for the ML inliner (it seems pretty benign compared to actual optimization passes). if it actually has some overhead, we shouldn't gate the detailed stats on a `cl::opt EnableDetailedFunctionProperties`, but on a bool specified by whoever queries FunctionPropertiesAnalysis, so the ML inliner can look for non-detailed stats and the stats pass can look for all stats

comments.author: boomanaiden154
comments.body:
> it'd be nice to check if this actually has any compile time overhead for the ML inliner (it seems pretty benign compared to actual optimization passes). if it actually has some overhead, we shouldn't gate the detailed stats on a cl::opt EnableDetailedFunctionProperties, but on a bool specified by whoever queries FunctionPropertiesAnalysis, so the ML inliner can look for non-detailed stats and the stats pass can look for all stats

Making it a pass parameter sounds like it would be reasonable enough to me.
